title: Who am I Sense of Self In Evangelion
display_title: Who am I Sense of Self In Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: Who am I? [Sense of Self In Evangelion]             Quote Post by TheSophist » Thu May 23, 2013 8:20 pm Rei: has no sense of self. Relies on other people, especially Gendo to define her wishes and desires. She pilots because of the wish of another. She has no self, thats why she comes across as alienated and un-human: she has no definitive desires. Asuka: you are a puppet! I hate you! Shinji: Has a sense of self (as a pilot) but is very anxious about it and does not identify with it Asuka: has an absolute sense of self, an absolute identity, but one that is entirely dependent on something external to herself (her ability to be a pilot) Each pilot represents how one can approach the nature of one's self. Can the show be summarised as the development of understanding how one chooses to be? The show is basically one big metaphor for psychological therapy, namely 'instrumentality'. where we all become perfect. We are forced to learn how people view us and how we view them. When all hearts and desires become fully understood to one another, there remains only free will, the choice of who you will become and what you will do. You are not dependent upon the demands of others (like Rei and Shinji intially, but not Asuka). But neither are you dependent upon external realities with which to create an identity for yourself (in the case of Asuka) Is anno just trying to describe therapy in anime form?

[]: Re: Who am I?             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Thu May 23, 2013 9:05 pm TheSophist wrote: The show is basically one big metaphor for psychological therapy, namely 'instrumentality'. where we all become perfect.

[]: Re: Who am I?             Quote Post by TheSophist » Thu May 23, 2013 9:38 pm ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: This may not be too far off the mark. The show was building to a big "instrumentality" ending from the very start, long before the production difficulties. Instrumentality as therapy could be very close to the original intention. But in this case, what are we to make of Shinji's decision at the end of Instrumentality in EoTV? Has he accepted instrumentality and remained in complementation, or has instrumentality simply completed him and now he and everyone else leaves the unified state? Despite the difficulties here, I do think that the idea of Instrumentality as being "therapy in anime form" is one which has merit and which needs to be explored when discussion the mechanics of Third Impact in EoTV.

[]:             Quote Post by xdiesp » Tue May 28, 2013 6:46 pm To me, instrumentality sounds like an easy metaphor for growing up. All of us had our instrumentality in development. One cuts the crap (err "detaches the umbilical cable") and enters society: 1) and gets away from games, from anime 2) gets away from the magic independency of infancy (just like a kid, Shinji wants to be independent but kids are not so, they need their parents to survive) 3) and starts dealing with group dynamics and being part of a whole The result is reality: bland but real. Of course if you want to keep watching your show, you choose to refure reality

[]: Re: Who am I? [Sense of Self In Evangelion]             Quote Post by Charsi » Tue May 28, 2013 7:54 pm TheSophist wrote: Rei: has no sense of self. Relies on other people, especially Gendo to define her wishes and desires. She pilots because of the wish of another. She has no self, thats why she comes across as alienated and un-human: she has no definitive desires.

[]:             Quote Post by Shinoyami65 » Wed May 29, 2013 5:06 am Self is a pretty huge concept in NGE. One aspect that particularly sticks out to me is that each character has a "self" which is seen by others as well as a private "inner self" which is only revealed under stress or during Instrumentality. Asuka, for example, tries to portray herself as a bubbly, desirable schoolgirl and competent pilot, as well as often reiterating her dislike of "baka Shinji". In actuality she is very insecure about herself and tries to use her piloting skills to substantiate her existence. The discrepancy between her "outer self" and "inner self" during the mindrape sequence in Episode 22: Episode 22  SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by AlexanderSoryuu » Fri May 31, 2013 12:42 pm "Everyone is alone. Everyone is empty. People no longer have need of others. For every talent there is a spare one. Every relationship can be replaced. I have gotten bored of a world like that." "Every single person fears that darkness in their heart and lives life trying to escape from it, to banish it. Even though it will never disappear as long as we remain human." Emptiness and solitude are the result of running away. We run away because we are afraid of what we are. No one wants to see themselves for what they really are. No one wants to break free and find his reason for existence. No one. They much more prefer using and exploiting, running and hiding. Selfishness is the way of humans. How can you love yourself if you're afraid of yourself? How can you love others if you don't love yourself? Shinji's decision in EoTV is nothing but a happy ending for Anno himself. On it's own, humanity has no hope for salvation, no one will break free. Only in death can one be completely free. That is the meaning of Kaworu's death. Only in complete, biological unity can humans function together, that is what Gendo believes. I lean towards Kaworu's way, but I understand Gendo's vision as well. Death may create nothing, but it's better than the New World we're living in. If you can't find your reason for existence, then why do you live? It's impossible to find it without realizing your true self. You don't even bother to realize it. So why do you live? You can't even kill yourself because only by realizing your reason for existence, you can choose to end it. Instrumentality is no therapy. It's the solution. The only way of being free in Shin Sekai. But ultimately, it's also a prison. Breaking free from Instrumentality, the only 'achievable' form of artificial freedom ( The one through death is impossible to achieve because we are livestock ) = Ultimate freedom. NGE cast did it. That's the whole point of Evangelion, to break free from the fear of yourself and the others. To accept yourself. But it's impossible in our world. No one would break free. No one would even accept Instrumentality as a solution. We are the New world, not the children of it. We made it. We don't want to be free of it. We want to be slaves. Thus, NGE remains a dream.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Fri May 31, 2013 3:39 pm I...REALLY don't think Anno intended for Instrumentality to be seen as a positive solution.

[]:             Quote Post by AlexanderSoryuu » Fri May 31, 2013 3:50 pm AuraTwilight wrote: I...REALLY don't think Anno intended for Instrumentality to be seen as a positive solution.

[]:             Quote Post by CX1329 » Fri May 31, 2013 4:43 pm I'd just like to add that Instrumentality is something that would -seriously- infuriate me in real life. Take your evolution of humanity and your unity somewhere else. I'd much rather have my flaws and my insecurities, but continue to exist as an individual, than willingly partake in someone's collectivist wet dream. Jesus.

[]:             Quote Post by Katana » Fri May 31, 2013 4:51 pm CX1329 wrote: I'd just like to add that Instrumentality is something that would -seriously- infuriate me in real life. Take your evolution of humanity and your unity somewhere else. I'd much rather have my flaws and my insecurities, but continue to exist as an individual, than willingly partake in someone's collectivist wet dream. Jesus.

[]:             Quote Post by CX1329 » Fri May 31, 2013 5:04 pm Katana wrote: Even though you'd be free from pain? even though you wouldn't be able suffer anymore, so we'd comfort each other?

[]:             Quote Post by AlexanderSoryuu » Fri May 31, 2013 5:07 pm CX1329 wrote: I'd just like to add that Instrumentality is something that would -seriously- infuriate me in real life. Take your evolution of humanity and your unity somewhere else. I'd much rather have my flaws and my insecurities, but continue to exist as an individual, than willingly partake in someone's collectivist wet dream. Jesus.

[]:             Quote Post by CX1329 » Fri May 31, 2013 5:37 pm AlexanderSoryuu wrote: I respect your opinion but what your comment is basically saying is that you prefer to live in someone's collectivist wet dream as long as you believe that you're an individual, in contrast to accepting to live in such dream of your own will. If you really preferred being an individual, you'd accept Instrumentality, see what it is and decide if you want to leave it or not.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Sat Jun 01, 2013 12:45 pm AlexanderSoryuu, Katana, you do both realize that Instrumentality is explicitly described as the ending of all individual consciousness, right? We don't just comfort and support each other in an illusionary perfect dream world, we literally all cease to be separate people and become one individual. There wouldn't be an "AlexanderSoryuu," a "Katana," a "CX1329," or an "AuraTwilight." There would just be the thing born from our destruction. It'd have our memories, maybe, but it wouldn't have our personalities, or emotions. It's a being that feels none of those things because it no longer feels pain or pleasure, joy or despair.

[]:             Quote Post by AlexanderSoryuu » Sat Jun 01, 2013 1:12 pm AuraTwilight wrote: AlexanderSoryuu, Katana, you do both realize that Instrumentality is explicitly described as the ending of all individual consciousness, right? We don't just comfort and support each other in an illusionary perfect dream world, we literally all cease to be separate people and become one individual. There wouldn't be an "AlexanderSoryuu," a "Katana," a "CX1329," or an "AuraTwilight." There would just be the thing born from our destruction. It'd have our memories, maybe, but it wouldn't have our personalities, or emotions. It's a being that feels none of those things because it no longer feels pain or pleasure, joy or despair.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Sun Jun 02, 2013 1:17 pm I don't know Katana's views on it, but that isn't the point. Only by ending individual consciousness can you be free of fear which remains as long as you're human.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun Jun 02, 2013 6:54 pm AuraTwilight wrote: you do both realize that Instrumentality is explicitly described as the ending of all individual consciousness, right?

[]:             Quote Post by Shinoyami65 » Sun Jun 02, 2013 7:44 pm NemZ wrote: That aside, why should we feel obligated to take Anno's word for it? I feel that rejecting instrumentality in EoE was a mistake... at that point humanity's already sold everything they own (including themselves) to buy a ticket to somewhere new, so what is there to be gained by deciding not to board the train? World's still fucked either way so you might as well take a chance.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Mon Jun 03, 2013 12:59 pm Depends on which ending you're talking about. Yes everyone melts together in EoE, but in EoTV very clearly people do still have some degree of individuality.
